data.text:
A young and successful executive was driving down a neighbourhood street, going a bit too fast in his new Jaguar.
He was watching for kids darting out from between parked cars and slowed down when he thought he saw something.
As his car passed, no children appeared. Instead, a brick smashed into the Jag's side door! He slammed on the brakes and backed the Jag back to the spot where the brick had been thrown.
The angry driver then jumped out of the car, grabbed the nearest kid and pushed him up against a parked car shouting-
'What was that all about and who are you? Just what the heck are you doing? That's a new car and that brick you threw is going to cost a lot of money. Why did you do it?'
The young boy was apologetic.
'Please, mister... please, I'm sorry but I didn't know what else to do,' he pleaded. 'I threw the brick because no one else would stop..'
With tears dripping down his face and off his chin, the youth pointed to a spot just around a parked car. 'It's my brother,' he said. 'He rolled off the curb and fell out of his wheelchair and I can't lift him up.'
Now sobbing, the boy asked the stunned executive, 'Would you please help me get him back into his wheelchair? 'He's hurt and he's too heavy for me.'
Moved beyond words, the driver tried to swallow the rapidly swelling lump in his throat...He hurriedly lifted the handicapped boy back into the wheelchair, then took out a linen handkerchief and dabbed at his fresh scrapes and cuts.
A quick look told him everything was going to be okay.
'Thank you and may God bless you,' the grateful child told the stranger, too shook up for words.
The man simply watched the boy push his wheelchair bound brother down the sidewalk towards their home.
It was a long, slow walk back to the Jaguar. The damage was very noticeable, but the driver never bothered to repair the dented side door.
He kept the dent there to remind himself of this message: 'Don't go through life so fast that someone has to throw a brick at you to get your attention!'
God whispers in our souls and speaks to our hearts.
Sometimes when we don't have time to listen, He has to throw a brick at us. It's our choice to listen or not.

data.post:
BJP getting 240 in 2024 may turn out to be the best thing that happened to Indian politics.

It reminded everyone of a fundamental truth:

Elections are not won in the air. They are won on the ground.

Not with slogans like "India Shining" or "Abki Baar 400 Paar".

They are won booth by booth, karyakarta by karyakarta, voter by voter.

The 2024 result was a harsh but necessary lesson that micro-execution often matters more than macro-narrative.

And the impact is already visible.

From Haryana to Maharashtra, Bihar and now Bengal, we are seeing a party that has gone back to the basics of electioneering. The shock of 2024 appears to have transformed the BJP into an even stronger ground-level election machine.

Ironically, had BJP crossed 300 on its own, or NDA reached 350-400, complacency might have set in. The opposition could well have been governing some of these states today.

Sometimes setback paves the way for greater success 

What looked like a setback in 2024 may ultimately become the turning point that extends BJP's political dominance for years to come.

A reminder from life itself:

When we think God is punishing us, He may actually be preparing us.

media.description:
An ode to cricket - II

People (the earlier gen) had thought after gavaskar and kapil Indian their interest in cricket will come down and then came sachin in 1989 and changed everything. 

T20 wasn't exactly working in India and in came dhoni and won the 1st T20 wc followed by IPL and the game changed forever

After 2011 WC and Sachin's imminent retirement many felt chalo ab kya hi bacha hai (remember missing office and crying my heart out on the day Sachin retired) but then Virat and Rohit under Dhoni ensured that we stick on. The 2023 wc loss (it still hurts) seemed like the end of it and then within a year they bounced back with 2024 T20 world cup.
Then came Gambhir to ensure that all heroism comes to an end with V and R forced retirements from tests and many of us too started moving on but then comes this 15 year old lad suryavanshi who gets the disengaged ones likes us back into the game vying for him and wishing that RR wins the IPL even though none other than Virat is on the other side.

Suryavanshi is the voice of cricket telling people like us that

Abhi na jao chhor ke
Ke dil abhi bhara nahi

data.post:
Everyone is blaming the politicians who took the centrestage and the organisers who could not even hire an event manager to manage such a huge event. 

But what about the public the people what gave them the right to put the city the state to shame and this is not the 1st time it started with ashok kumar nite, then when gavaskar swore never to play at eden again and then the 1996 semifinal fiasco at eden and again the 1999 test match against Pakistan. The public needs to be know that the greens belongs to the players and the ground deserves respect. Throwing bottles breaking chairs is hooliganism, the videos of people destroying public property at salt lake is distasteful and horrifying but is hardly getting the condemnation it deserves. 

I feel happy 2023 final of ODI world cup was not held at Eden âââotherwise fans would have created one more embarrassment. 

I have always maintained it is the people of bengal the voters in particular who are responsible for the royal mess âthe state is into. data.post:
India has always been a land of heroes and devotion. We are a civilization where people rally around a central force, where leadership and inspiration flow from individuals who rise above the ordinary. Western notions of absolute equality sound ideal on paper but rarely work in the real world. Nature itself shows us harmony, not sameness. Even the planets differ in size, yet they remain part of one system.

Our cultural DNA is built on the concept of Bhakti. Nowhere else does the divine take form through countless expressions and personalities like in India. Here, inspiration is personal. Here, greatness is not diluted in the name of uniformity.

Whenever someone tries to turn everything into a mechanical structure that values process over passion, India resists. Cricket is no different. Recently, we saw a reminder of what truly moves this country. After the century, that fan didnât rush in for a hug. He fell at Viratâs feet. That moment wasnât about stardom. It was about devotion meeting excellence. It was India being India.

We thrive on superstars. On those who can carry an entire teamâs spirit, not just bits-and-pieces players who neither dominate a role nor elevate others. Leaders inspire. Icons unite. That is how Indian cricket has always reached its peaks.

Thank you, Virat, for showing the path once again. I hope the powers that be have understood the message. We must move forward with the spirit of our heroes, not get stuck in another Greg Chappell era.

 An avid cricket fan

data.post:
Jeevan ka 10wa ras - Holi

Certain words are beyond translation hurdang (à¤¹à¥à¤¡à¤¼à¤¦à¤à¤) is one such word neither ruckus nor running riot or behaving wild - none of these do justice to this word. 

I call it the 10th ras beyond the 9 rasas (navras) - Shringara (beauty), Hasya (laughter), Karuna (empathy), Raudra (anger), Veer (courage), Bhaya (fear), Bibhatsa (disgust), Adbhut (wonder), and Shanti (peace). 

These navras are part and parcel of our life but somwhere deep inside there is a child in us who wants to break out of all the social barriers and etiquettes and wants to indulge in - u guessed it right hurdang (à¤¹à¥à¤¡à¤¼à¤¦à¤à¤). 

Perhaps out of this insight was born this festival of colors called holi. A day when niceties take a backseat and thr inner child expresses itself for few hours least bothered by what anyone will think as everyone have suspended their judgemental nature for some time (wish we could be like this more often and the world would be a more joyful place). 

On this day of Holi lets give our sensibilities some rest and flow with flow of these wonderful unique festival our culture has blessed us with. Let the inner child in you find its true expression. 

 Apne bachpan ko wapas bulaayein
 Holi ka tyohar khul. Kar manayei

media.title: Mobile uploads
media.description:
Note on Radha ashtami

Radha ashtami the janamdin of Radhaji comes 15 days after krishna janamashtami. Radha is actually elder than Krishna and it said 

Jo Radha janam na hoto 
To krishna virano roto
Na yo hoto krishna avatar 

In short there would hv been no krishna without Radha 

Our ancient culture (and I refuse to call it mythology) is full of examples of equal importance given to both genders there is ardhanadheswara (half man half woman) of shiva mohini roop of vishnu and same is krishna and Radha. Radha is the feminine side of Krishna. 

Even when we look at trimurti of brahma Vishnu Mahesh each has a feminine presence without which they cannot perform their roles effectively. The creator in brahma needs to knowledge Goddess Saraswati. 
The nurturer (palanhar) in Vishnu requires wealth for sustenance hence Goddess Lakshmi and the finisher in Mahadev requires power hence Parvati the Goddess of Shakti. 

Still above knowledge above wealth and above power is something that truly runs this world and that is LOVE and Radha is the epitome of love for Krishna the best (shrest) among men (nar). 

On this auspicious day of Radha Ashtami let's bow to the Sriji or radhe or shree radhe - the Goddess of love, tenderness, compassion, devotion in short an epitome of feminine qualities. 

Also let's bow to our wonderful culture which has so much respect for the feminine contrary to what we have been made to believe. 

Shree radhe shree radhe shree radhe

data.post:
An appeal to the citizen of our city/state 

I remember in 2016 the Posta under construction flyover had fallen and every Kolkatans heart was aching due to the enormity of the man made tragedy.

During the same time India was playing the semi final of the T20 world cup and the final was to happen in eden gardens. It was the only time in my life that there was a sort of relief when India lost the semi final for it wud hv been really difficult to cheer for India in the aftermath of such a humungous tragedy.

As the citizen of a nation or state or city or society we share a collective conscience which at times demands that we put aside our personal joys and revellries aside and adhere to call of this common conscience to be with the sufferers of the tragedy whom we may not know but are connected thru this collective conscience. 

Being human shud be above all.

The above post is inspired by a similar post by Roopendra Narayan Roy

media.description:
A tribute to Dravid

If cricket is a gentleman's game then that gentleman has to be Rahul Dravid. Can't think of another person who has carried himself with the same poise, elegance, dedication and commitment like he has done. But did cricket serve him what he deserved - not really. The biggest and perhaps the only major success (as a team) of his life came on the last day of his association with cricket - both life and cricket at times can be really testing and cruel and the it's best students are put through the most stringent tests. And when life tests us let's a be dravid and not a Gandhi (dig intended).

Thank you Mr Dravid for showing us that life can he lived like this too that game can be played like this too both as a player and as a coach and the more committed you are more it will test you but there is redemption at the end sometimes at the very end.

If I had a son i would have liked him to become like rahul Dravid in whatever vocation/profession he chose.

Quoting shakespeare

His life was gentle; and the elements
So mixed in him, that Nature might stand up
And say to all the world, THIS WAS A MAN!

That man to me/us is Rahul Dravid

media.description:
An ode to cricket 

People say cricket is a religion
Actually for us the now grown up kids of the 90s cricket is an emotion.

It is a memory that we live relive endlessly and every time we play the recording in our head there is fountain of joy that bursts. 

When sachin hit Qadir for those 3 6s in Pakistan
When jaedja hit 40 runs of 2 overs in wc
When Prasad got Sohail out
When Sachin hit the mighty aussies out of the park at sharjah
When sachin scored 140 against Kenya after his father's death
When ganguly waved his t shirt at lords
When India lost the 2003 wc final
Then the loss against bdesh in 2007
Dhoni and co winning the maiden 2007 T20
2011 wc win with that 6 from dhoni.....

We the kids of 90s feel we hv grown up and outgrown cricket but then come these moments that bring back the childlike joy out of us.

When at midnight u shout like a kid when klaasen and miller get out (I actuslly did to bring my daughter out of their stupor) the same way u did when prasad bowled Sohail inn1996. And it is in these moments that yr heart says

 Abhi mujhme kahi Baaki thodi si hai zindagi.

Thank u cricket for letting the child in us be alive and kicking and reminding us from time to time

 An adult is nothing but an overgrown child

In a life otherwise full of the dull and mundane with some moments of individuals highs and lows, cricket is one thing that brings unforgettable  collective joy and happiness across the country. Experiencing live the impromptu celebrations on streets of Kolkata was a such a moment of joy.

Thank u cricket for no other aspect of life has given us more joy than you.

data.text:
A young and successful executive was driving down a neighbourhood street, going a bit too fast in his new Jaguar.
He was watching for kids darting out from between parked cars and slowed down when he thought he saw something.
As his car passed, no children appeared. Instead, a brick smashed into the Jag's side door! He slammed on the brakes and backed the Jag back to the spot where the brick had been thrown.
The angry driver then jumped out of the car, grabbed the nearest kid and pushed him up against a parked car shouting-
'What was that all about and who are you? Just what the heck are you doing? That's a new car and that brick you threw is going to cost a lot of money. Why did you do it?'
The young boy was apologetic.
'Please, mister... please, I'm sorry but I didn't know what else to do,' he pleaded. 'I threw the brick because no one else would stop..'
With tears dripping down his face and off his chin, the youth pointed to a spot just around a parked car. 'It's my brother,' he said. 'He rolled off the curb and fell out of his wheelchair and I can't lift him up.'
Now sobbing, the boy asked the stunned executive, 'Would you please help me get him back into his wheelchair? 'He's hurt and he's too heavy for me.'
Moved beyond words, the driver tried to swallow the rapidly swelling lump in his throat...He hurriedly lifted the handicapped boy back into the wheelchair, then took out a linen handkerchief and dabbed at his fresh scrapes and cuts.
A quick look told him everything was going to be okay.
'Thank you and may God bless you,' the grateful child told the stranger, too shook up for words.
The man simply watched the boy push his wheelchair bound brother down the sidewalk towards their home.
It was a long, slow walk back to the Jaguar. The damage was very noticeable, but the driver never bothered to repair the dented side door.
He kept the dent there to remind himself of this message: 'Don't go through life so fast that someone has to throw a brick at you to get your attention!'
God whispers in our souls and speaks to our hearts.
Sometimes when we don't have time to listen, He has to throw a brick at us. It's our choice to listen or not.

data.text:
Yr morning begins at 7 PM (not AM) 

If u are serious about waking up early:

> set a 7 PM alarm (yes it is 7 PM not AM)
> try and finish yr dinner btwn 7 to 8 pm
> set yr morning alarm for the time u wish to get up
> go to bed 7/8 hours before the alarm time u hv set (if yr alarm is set at 6 AM bed time is 10/11 pm)

It works. Try it out and share yr results.

Happy mornings.

media.description:
How Ram can help in making tough decisions

Ravana had a boon that he could take any form he wanted.

One day his minister came running to him and said - why don't u use yr boon and become like Ram and Sita will fall for u.

Ravan gave him a grave look (saying I was waiting for u to gv me this idea) and said.

I already tried what you said but the moment I transformed into Ram's form the only woman I could think about was my wife Mandodari.

That's the power of the character (charitra) of Ram. In our life we all have seen just by playing the character of Ram how arun govil's life completely changed.

Coming to the heading next time you face a moral dilemma in life mentally think

If I was Ram what i would have done the answer you would get is the truth, the right, the dharma, whether u follow it or not is upto you.

Jai shree Ram.

data.post:
Spirituality and Motivation

Spirituality is not supposed to motivate

It is *to take away* the need for motivation to do what is right/required irrespective of outcomes, irrespective of personal likes/dislikes, irrespective of whether the mind and body are willing or not.

Spiritual practices are an antidote to the need for motivation and not a source of motivation.

The independence of India has been widely attributed to Quit India movement but it was the Navy Mutiny at Bombay in 1946 which led to Britsh leaving India. British PM Atlee was informed that Netaji has created such a wave of nationalism that if British do not leave India the life of Britishers in India is endangered. read more here --> t.ly/ZRtjW

Also, Netaji was 1st PM of undivided India and had taken oath as PM on October 21, 1943

His role in getting independence is highly under appreciated.

media.title: Photos
media.description:
How our gen is unique in so many ways 

We are the last generation which has seen life 

- from doordarshan to satellite to OTT
- from one landline phone to 2 sim cards in every mobile
- life before and after not only internet but we also see life before and after AI
- india winning 2 ODI WC 

we even saw thru the corona kaal but yet in the twilight years of our life we shall be most proud that we saw this day when 496 years of wait got over, 
how many generations before us waited to see this day but we have been the chosen one, much of our gen next is underprepared to realise the enormity of this piece of history that they are seeing pass by (busy on their phones) and in many ways our previous generation and we are the fortunate ones to experience, understand and realise the value of what we have been witnessed.

Let the happiness, the celebrations and the after effects of witnessing such a historic event continue to linger in our hearts.

media.description:
Krishna is known and reverred for so many things he did 

Ram is reverred for so many things he did not do

1. At the time of vanvas Dasharath asked him to revolt against him and take the throne - Ram didn't 

2. Laxman asked him to use his bow and arrow to stop the injustice meted out to him - he didn't 

3. His nanihal was very close to the jungle where he was staying never took any support from them

4. Bali had ravan under his belt, ram could hv taken his help and got Sita back but he rather killed bali as bali had forcibly overtaken his brother sugriv's wife and taking help of such a person would be against dharma - so Ram didn't take Bali's help

5. When sita was kidnapped all he needed to do was ask Bharat for help but he was in exile and it was his personal battle - Bharat was not even aware of the battle ram was fighting - didn't take help of his own brother 

6. After killing of bali and coronation of sugriv (around the month of June) the army was ready and willing to move to lanka but ram stopped it saying that monsoon had set in and waited for 4 months for chaumasa (rainy season) to get over - Ram ka dhairya

7. Could hv overtaken the kingdom from sugriv and vibhishan but didn't and made them kings. Till date India is the only major country which has never attacked or overtaken any other country. 

It is this quality of ram of doing the right things and avoiding shortcuts which we worship.

2. Laxman asked him to use his bow and arrow to stop the injustice meted out to him - he didn't 

3. His nanihal was very close to the jungle where he was staying never took any support from them

4. Bali had ravan under his belt, ram could hv taken his help and got Sita back but he rather killed bali as bali had forcibly overtaken his brother sugriv's wife and taking help of such a person would be against dharma - so Ram didn't take Bali's help

5. When sita was kidnapped all he needed to do was ask Bharat for help but he was in exile and it was his personal battle - Bharat was not even aware of the battle ram was fighting - didn't take help of his own brother 

6. After killing of bali and coronation of sugriv (around the month of June) the army was ready and willing to move to lanka but ram stopped it saying that monsoon had set in and waited for 4 months for chaumasa (rainy season) to get over - Ram ka dhairya

7. Could hv overtaken the kingdom from sugriv and vibhishan but didn't and made them kings. Till date India is the only major country which has never attacked or overtaken any other country. 

It is this quality of ram of doing the right things and avoiding shortcuts which we worship.

media.description:
She is a Muslim woman shah bano. Surprisingly she indirectly played a key role in ram mandir movement. In 1985, the supreme court of India ruled in favour of Shah Bano that she would be entitled to maintenance beyond the period of 89 days (iddat period). The then Rajiv Gandhi govt brought in legislation to overturn this SC decision. Now to do a balancing act RG govt allowed the opening of locks and Puja in ram mandir/babri masjid. This led to the uprise of ram janam bhumi movement. So in effect this Muslim woman played a key role in starting the RJB movement. Jai ho

https://x.com/SortedEagle/status/1168461058979160064?s=20

Interview of Arif mohd khan currently governor of Kerala, who was part of the then rajiv Gandhi govt in 1985 and had resigned protesting against stand taken by RG govt in shah bano case.

Also read this
https://kreately.in/shah-bano-to-ram-mandir-a-sordid-tale-of-opportunism/

media.description:
Radha ashtami

Radha ashtami the janamdin of Radhaji comes 15 days after krishna janamashtami. Radha is actually elder than Krishna and it said 

Jo Radha janam na hoto 
To krishna virano roto
Na yo hoto krishna avatar 

In short there would hv been no krishna without Radha 

Our ancient culture (and I refuse to call it mythology) is full of examples of equal importance given to both genders there is ardhanadheswara (half man half woman) of shiva mohini roop of vishnu and same is krishna and Radha. Radha is the feminine side of Krishna. 

Even when we look at trimurti of brahma Vishnu Mahesh each has a feminine presence without which they cannot perform their roles effectively. The creator in brahma needs to knowledge Goddess Saraswati. 
The nurturer (palanhar) in Vishnu requires wealth for sustenance hence Goddess Lakshmi and the finisher in Mahadev requires power hence Parvati the Goddess of Shakti. 

Still above knowledge above wealth and above power is something that truly runs this world and that is LOVE and Radha is the epitome of love for Krishna the best (shrest) among men (nar). 

On this auspicious day of Radha Ashtami let's bow to the Sriji or radhe or shree radhe - the Goddess of love, tenderness, compassion, devotion in short an epitome of feminine qualities. 

Also let's bow to our wonderful culture which has so much respect for the feminine contrary to what we have been made to believe. 

Shree radhe shree radhe shree radhe

data.post:
How to get 13 months of life in a year. 

Reduce sleep quota by 2 hours daily 

365 x 2 = 730/24 hv = 30.41 days that's like 1 extra year every 12 years 

How to reduce sleep by 2 hours 

Stream line the other 3 essential processes 

1. Breathing - the most essential life process but the path to life (the nostrils) are perennially jam. Yoga pranayam, walking running, exercising, using steam etc improve the process of breathing in turn it improves the metabolism which in turn prepares the body to release more energy with less food. So breathing well will reduce food intake 

Drinking - Drink more water (remain hydrated) many times when we feel we are hungry we are actually thirsty/unhydrated and confuse it with hunger. So drink more to eat less 

Eating - the smallest and easiest diet plan is 'eat less'. Improving breathing and drinking aids eating less, apart from this chewong more and mindful/distraction free eating also help in eating less 

Most of the stress in life is a result of imbalance in the 4 essential interrelated life processes. So in summary remains stress free by

Breathing better 
Remaining  hydrated 
Eating less 
Sleeping less

data.post:
Happy 50th birthday to the icon Sachin Tendulkar.

If there is anyone who has brought most joy to people since 1989 it is SRT - from hitting abdul Qadir for those 6s in 1989 to taming the world champions australia in 1998 at sharjah to the 136 at cheapuk against Pakistan to returning back after his father's funeral and scoring 140 against Kenya to taking India  to 2003 wc final to the heart break of 2007 and then on 2md April 2011 winning the ODI WC and finally 100 100s.
The journey of many many lives including mine would not have been the same without Him

I remember in 1998 Outlook magazine's year end edition read the following about sachin

In a year of gloom one man brought us cheer.

Will say the same 

In our life with lots of gloom this one man brought us lots of cheer 

Once again wishing a very happy birthday to the Joy of India.
Sharing a video from India today conclave 

https://youtu.be/kRofNOkZg0M

data.post:
U shud drop everything else and read this.

Shrimati Draupadi Murmuâs personal life is a story of resilience and âself-prideâ. There was an unforgettable moment for her in 2009 when her 25-year-old son Lakshman died unexpectedly after attending a gathering in Bhubaneshwar. His death broke her. Supriya Kumari, head of the Brahma Kumari ashram in Rairangpaur, Odisha says, âShe was completely shattered. She wasnât having any life within her to even talk.â

In one of the talks on Brahma Kumariâs television programme, Draupadi Murmu herself narrated this event, âThe tsunami came into my life in 2009. It was a huge jolt to me. I couldnât hear anything for a few days. I slipped into depression. Log kehte the ye to mar jaegi (People thought I will not survive). But, no, I wanted to live.â

After two months, she visited Supriya Kumari in the Brahma Kumaris ashram, completed a course and learnt Sahaj Rajyog. She recovered by changing her life. Since then, *she gets up every day around 3.30 am* and retires to *bed by 9.30 pm*. She *does yoga and meditation unfailingly and is punctual as well*. The spiritual bent of life not only survived her but stabilized her too.

But tragedy befell Murmu again when her younger son, Shipun, died in a road accident. When his body was brought home, she was broken completely, once again.

Rajesh Sharma, a local journalist who was present at her home, says, âShe was crying uncontrollably. She broke down before her sonâs body. She raised her hands to the sky, asking, âGod, what more do you want from me? What is left now?ââ The catastrophe came in droves.

In an indescribable series of events, her mother and a younger brother died within a month. And a year later, due to deep depression, her husband, Shyam Charan Murmu, died as well.

At that point, Droupadi Murmu told a TV anchor with pain in her voice: âWhen my second son died, the jolt was a little less than before because I was doing meditation. My husband wasnât as strong as me, so he couldnât survive.â She insisted on her only daughter, Itishree, to marry and lead a normal life. *After the deaths of five family members, she turned to spirituality and vegetarianism*. When she was the governor of Jharkhand (2015-2021), she made the kitchen completely vegetarian. She is likely to bring many fundamental changes in the running of the government in the Rashtrapati Bhavan if she becomes the President.

Draupadi Murmu has *donated her familyâs land in Pahadpur for public causes*. She runs the SLS residential school in memory of her husband and two sons. At the exact location, she has made samadhis in their memory. Itâs a heart-wrenching sight.

At the same time when you see tribal girls and boys getting free education and decent surroundings around samadhis, you can visualise carving out a beautiful future out of a deathly past.

Take a bow Droupadi Murmu, to be the President of India!
~~~~Forwarded~~~~

They are not just satisfied with observing their own religions, but are determined to destroy all other religions. Thatâs why the only way to make peace with them is to embrace their religions"

Rabindra Nath Tagore, 

Original works of Rabindranath Vol. 24 page 375, Vishwa Bharti 

Further In a letter to Hemantabala Sarkar, written on the 16th of October, 1933, quoted in Bengali weekly âSwastikaâ on 21-6-1999, he says, âThe terrible situation of the country makes my mind restless and I cannot keep silent. Meaningless ritual keeps the Hindus divided into hundred sects. So we are suffering from a series of defeats. We are tired and worn-out by the fortunes by the internal-external enemies. The Muslims are united in religion and rituals. The Bengali Muslims the South Indian Muslims and even the Muslims outside India-all are united. They always stand united in face of danger. The broken and divided Hindus will not be able to combat them. Days are coming when the Hindus will be again humiliated by the Muslims. âYou are a mother of children, one day you will die, passing the future of Hindus society on the weak shoulders of your children, but think about their future.â

data.post:
Yr morning begins at 7 PM (not AM) 

If u are serious about waking up early:

> set a 7 PM alarm (yes it is 7 PM not AM)
> try and finish yr dinner btwn 7 to 8 pm
> set yr morning alarm for the time u wish to get up
> go to bed 7/8 hours before the alarm time u hv set (if yr alarm is set at 6 AM bed time is 10/11 pm)

It works. Try it out and share yr results.

Happy mornings.

data.post:
Just came thru this analysis by someone of the bgbs summit and why it is not likely to work. These words are not mine but won't say the same about my sentiments. 

Bengal is doomed. And Bengali Hindus are solely responsible for their callousness. 

What happened?

The Bengal Global Business Summit hosted by the state government ends (Thursday, April 21). Like the six earlier editions of this exercise, this one too will conclude with announcement of investment proposals worth lakhs of crores by industrialists.

The problem

Few of those proposals will actually see the light of the day. Thatâs because there are some systemic flaws in Bengal that keep investors away from the state.

1. Extortions

Ruling party politicians, criminals patronised by them and bureaucrats regularly demand huge sums of money from businesses. The recent case of IFB Agro is a good example of this.
Bengal is also notorious for âsyndicatesâ that force industrialists to source sub-standard raw materials at inflated rates from them.

2. Poor work-culture

Thereâs a popular slogan in Bengali--aashte jete beton chai, kaj korle bonus chai (we need salaries to report for work and bonus to actually work).

Capital started fleeing the state in the 1960s in response to militant trade unionism and the rise of communism. Work culture also took a nosedive and has been unable to recover since.

3. Adversarial relation with Union Govt

Another legacy of the Left which Mamata Banerjee has only accentuated. Her blind opposition to and frequent/unnecessary quarrels with New Delhi puts industrialists and businessmen in a an uncomfortable position.

Even at the start of the âsummitâ Wednesday, Banerjee displayed her antagonism towards the Centre, causing discomfort to industrialists, businessmen and even the diplomats present.

4. Political strife

Bengal has been notorious for political violence since the 1970s, and things have only turned worse under Banerjeeâs rule.

5. Poor governance and politicised state institutions

Poor governance has hobbled Bengal since 1977. Even the current chief minister is said to be whimsical and short-tempered, and that makes it difficult for industrialists and businessmen to deal with her or her colleagues as well as the state administration.

Also, with all institutions--the police, civil administration and justice-dispensing systems--heavily politicised, a fair enforcement of contract is difficult in Bengal.

6. âCapital-unfriendlyâ psyche 

Nurtured since childhood on an anti-capital diet, the Bengali psyche is unfriendly towards businessmen and industrialists. The complete takeover of the education system by communists has resulted in generations of Bengalis harbouring antipathy towards private generators of wealth.

Bottomline

Merely rolling out the red carpet for potential investors and painting a rosy picture of the state wonât do. These systemic flaws that hobble industries and businesses in Bengal, and keep investors away, must be addressed first.

data.text:
Five More Minutes!
 
 
While at the park one day, a woman sat down next to a man on a bench near a playground. 
âThatâs my son over there,â she said, pointing to a little boy in a red sweater who was gliding down the slide. 
âHeâs a fine looking boyâ the man said. âThatâs my daughter on the bike in the white dress.â 
Then, looking at his watch, he called to his daughter. âWhat do you say we go, Melissa?â 
Melissa pleaded, âJust five more minutes, Dad. Please? Just five more minutes!â 
The man nodded and Melissa continued to ride her bike to her heartâs content. Minutes passed and the father stood and called again to his daughter. âTime to go now?â 
Again Melissa pleaded, âFive more minutes, Dad. Just five more minutes!â 
The man smiled and said, âOK.â 
âMy God, you certainly are a patient father,â the woman responded. 
The man smiled and then said, âHer older brother Tommy was killed by a drunk driver last year while he was riding his bike near here. I never spent much time with Tommy and now Iâd give anything for just five more minutes with him. Iâve vowed not to make the same mistake with Melissa. 
She thinks she has five more minutes to ride her bike. The truth is, I get five more minutes to watch her play.â 
Life is all about making priorities, what are your priorities?
Give someone you love 5 more minutes of your time today!

data.text:
In a mother's womb were two babies. One asked the other: "Do you believe in life after delivery?" The other replies, "why, of course. There has to be something after delivery. Maybe we are here to prepare ourselves for what we will be later. "Nonsense," says the other. "There is no life after delivery. What would that life be?" "I don't know, but there will be more light than here. Maybe we will walk with our legs and eat from our mouths."
The other says "This is absurd! Walking is impossible. And eat with our mouths? Ridiculous. The umbilical cord supplies nutrition. Life after delivery is to be excluded. The umbilical cord is too short." "I think there is something and maybe it's different than it is here." the other replies, "No one has ever come back from there. Delivery is the end of life, and in the after-delivery it is nothing but darkness and anxiety and it takes us nowhere."
"Well, I don't know," says the other, "but certainly we will see mother and she will take care of us." "Mother??" You believe in mother? Where is she now? "She is all around us. It is in her that we live. Without her there would not be this world." "I don't see her, so it's only logical that she doesn't exist." To which the other replied, "sometimes when you're in silence you can hear her, you can perceive her." I believe there is a reality after delivery and we are here to prepare ourselves for that reality.

Resemblance..??? Anyone...??

data.text:
The Porcupine story on importance  of joint family/team work:

It was a particularly harrowing time in porcupine land. The winter was severe, and the porcupines were finding it difficult to survive, and freezing to death. That's when the porcupines decided to meet and agree on a course of action.
As they got together to discuss their survival strategy, they discovered that just by being close to each other they were able to feel warmer and protect each other. Being closeted together meant that their bodies generated heat which helped keep everybody warm. So they found they could survive the cold by just staying together.
But there was a problem. As they got closer to each other, they found their quills would poke and hurt. Feeling the discomfort, some porcupines decided to avoid the pain and moved away. But as they went out, the cold got them and they died.
Soon better sense prevailed, and the porcupines realised it was better to stay together and survive rather than go out on their own and die. Getting poked by the quills seemed like a small price to pay for survival.

data.post:
A road journey from Kumardhubi, a small company-owned township where I was brought up, in the western fringes of Bengal, to Calcutta, took you through what was then an undeterred colossal industrial estate. Kumardhubi itself hosted some leading engineering companies, including McNally Bharat, which my father built from its formative years, a foundry which was then Indiaâs largest and most specialised, and a fire-brick plant that was a frontrunner in its field. The ancient national highway that connected Indiaâs capital, Delhi, to its industrial power house, Calcutta, meandered through the towns of Kulti and Asansol with large manufacturing firms such as Indian Iron, British Oxygen, Carewâs Gin, Chittaranjan Locomotives, Bengal Coal, Pilkington Glass, Martin Burn, Sen Raleigh Cycles, amongst hundreds of smaller ancillary units employing millions of people.
As you drove eastwards, you crossed the towns of Raniganj with its huge collieries, previously privately owned and managed by erstwhile giants such as Bird & Company, Thapars and the Tatas. The highway cut through Durgapur, which had a massive steel plant, a fertiliser factory and dozens of smaller engineering and chemical plants. The journey continued through Burdwan, the then district headquarters, and you ultimately reached Uttarpara on the outskirts of Calcutta. This was a giant industrial hub with hundreds of factories, including Hindustan Motors.
Calcutta, historically, an imperial city with deep water ports like Kidderpore Docks and navigable rivers, was the industrial and business capital of the Asia Pacific region, several times the size of Hong Kong and Singapore. Asiaâs largest companies and banks were headquartered here. For instance, the Chartered Bank Building existed even before the merger of Standard and Chartered banks. Lyons Range hosted the stock exchange and the offices of leading insurance companies. In fact, the entire area of Dalhousie and Fairley Place contained the stalwarts of Indian business with names like Martin Burn, Andrew Yule, Bird & Company, Garden Reach, Jessops, Murphy Radio and several businesses across the spectrum from ship building to mining. Calcutta was a truly global city with a highly diversified demography that included Iraqi Jews, Armenians, Persians, Chinese and others who chose to make it their home. Commercial opportunities were considerable and so was the need for expertise. In Calcutta there was serious money to be made. Industries in Bengal extended north-eastwards into the tea gardens of Darjeeling and Jalpaiguri. These produced the finest tea in the world with auction prices that exceeded those of other produce from across the world.
The tragedy is not that all of this was subsequently destroyed; the tragedy is that it can never be rebuilt. And that is what happened to Calcutta and the entire industrial manufacturing of Bengal since the 1970s. Left Front governments, particularly under the leadership of Jyoti Basu, Bengalâs erstwhile Chief Minister, cracked down on industry and empowered unions to such an extent that factory after factory began to shut permanently. The red flags of the Communist Party unions fluttered across the state, creating a condition that made it literally impossible for business to function. Most shut shop, some were nationalised and the wiser ones quickly relocated their offices and plants to other locations. The only significant company that retains its head office in Calcutta today, at Virginia House, and continues to flourish, is ITC. Most of its operations, however, are now spread across other parts of India. None of the other giants exist anymore and if they do, are a tiny fraction of their original size.
Mr Basu, a graduate of University College, London, was called to the Bar at the Middle Temple. He was an activist in his student days and remained one for his entire political career which was propped by labour unions. He depended on them, as they did on him. Consequently, strikes and unrest became the order of the day and companies began to suffer. Many businessmen tried to hang on but, in the end, simply gave up. Bengal had become intolerable to function in. Law and order were replaced by anarchy and goons of the communist party routinely harassed business managers and owners. Millions of workers were laid off as business enterprise either shut or fled the tyranny of the communists. Bengal had not just lost its edge; it had simply lost everything.
Leading brands like Dunlops, Guest Keen Williams, Braithwaite, Burn & Company (after whom the town of Burnpur carries its name) and Metal Box were either nationalised or ceased to exist. Indian business families like the Birlas and Singhanias shifted to Bombay and other parts of India, closing factories and leading to economic ruin. This created massive unemployment. The Communist government added fuel to the fire when it prevented the police from interfering in labour disputes even when business managers were manhandled and beaten up by union goons. In 1984, as a young project engineer responsible for a project site in Durgapur, where my employer was contracted to build a material handling plant for Hindustan Fertiliser, I was made to stand on an oil barrel for four hours in the mid-afternoon sun, with the labour unions shouting threatening slogans. The odd part was, I had no clue as to what I had done wrong or indeed what their demands were. Strikes and violence in those years were as common as reporting to work.
Tragically, Bengal, a leader in industry and technology, reduced itself to a laggard state which no sensible businessmen would want to be associated with. Its unions had sent investors scampering away, never to return. The Communist Party and its leadership had ensured this fate. The great companies of Calcutta had closed shop forever. Subsequently, a reverse migration began to happen and educated Bengalis left their homes for better opportunities elsewhere. Ironically, six decades ago â all roads led to Calcutta; now they all lead away from it. The great industrial hub from Kumardhubi, across Asansol, Raniganj and Durgapur is now a rust belt. In Calcutta, factories that once produced products that were best in class have been replaced by retail showrooms. Dum Dum airport, once Indiaâs busiest, with direct links to most European and Asian cities, is now a shadow if its former self. Most global airlines have pulled out. From a world class metropolis that boasted the finest Christmas decorations along Chowrangee and Park Street, Calcutta stands reduced to a status of an over-crowded and wretched city.
The communists did realise their folly when Buddhadeb Bhattacharya became Chief Minister, but by then it was too late. Mr Bhattacharyaâs plans to revive Bengal, beginning with a chemical manufacturing hub in Nandigram, fell into political controversy. Thereafter, the failed Tata Motors plant in Singur, sealed the fate on any meaningful revival of investment in the state. Mamata Banerjee of the Trinamool Congress, whose anti-capitalist antics were responsible for the cultivated collapse of these projects, subsequently replaced Mr Bhattacharya. Last month, she assumed her third consecutive term in office, a telling reflection of the sort of interest groups from which she derives her political legitimacy.
Calcutta, upon which an empire had once been built, now lies reduced to just another fallen star. It has no credible prospect of returning to its former glory, at least in my life time. For me and an entire generation of Indians who remember that city for what it once was, that is a dreadful tragedy. Its demise, and indeed that of Bengal, is a clear example of the consequences of disrespect for wealth and those who create it. It is an important lesson for politicians. When governments create hurdles for business, it undermines their growth and productivity. When they ill-treat them, it leads to a flight of capital and cessation of economic activity; unemployment and unrest inevitably follows. That, in a nutshell, is Bengalâs history of the last 40 years. Oddly, many policy planners still donât get it.

data.post:
The festival of diwali as per our history books in 2031

In todayâs times while the older generation associates the Festival of Lights with the Moghuls, fondly calling it Jashn-e-Chirag, the younger generation associates the festival with the homecoming of the Bollywood superstar Aryan Khan after a state-sponsored persecution in which he was incarcerated for months. An ardent group of fans welcomed him with wax candles and earthen lamps, and this, in turn, became an annual symbol of the Festival of Lights. The fact that Aryan Khan, just like his father, has risen high in a demanding industry without anyoneâs help only adds to his appeal as the mascot of the festival

data.post:
Seeing the fate of psephologists election after election still daring to make some political predictions

1. TMC is going to win the elections for sure (till 1st of May) 

2. Exit polls will give 140 to 180 seats to tmc and rajdeep will be seen dancing again in the  studios during the exit polls (remember bihar exit polls) 

3. On 2nd May watch ndtv where Dr. Prannoy Roy will tell u where their predictions went wrong and role aimim and furfura sharif and everyone else played (except bhaipo). During the show pls make it a point to enjoy the sullen faces of other anchors and the panelists

4. Also the attention which was fully on didi till 1 may will suddenly shift to shuvendu adhikari for the nandigram result.

5. The word anti incumbency will be used do often that it will appear that its a fate accompli

6. No one will compare the exit poll vs actual results on 2 may

7. By the evening in muted voices the role of evm will be discussed but the loyalty towards kejriwal will ensure that it remains muted

And, hopefully by the evening of 2nd May my state  will see the onset of a new era and set out on a growth path it always deserved but never got bcoz of the foolish choices of its electorate for the last 4 decades.

data.text: Pawan Lohia added a new photo.

In continuation of my previous post the question is how to express the divinity and Swami Vivekananda answered it thus:

Each soul is potentially divine. The goal is to manifest this divinity by controlling nature, external and internal. Do this either by work, or worship, or psychic control, or philosophy - by one, or more, or all of these - and be free. This is the whole of religion. Doctrines, or dogmas, or rituals, or books, or temples, or forms, are but secondary details.

Some excellent food for thought!!!

Note on timings for diwali puja
Dhanteras 
Today dhanteras will set in after 930 pm so dhanteras diya pujan shud be done after 930 PM

People who do dhanteras puja in office are advised  do it on Friday 13 Nov  before 5:58:35 PM when dhanteras ends. 

Choti Diwali/chaturdashi
Choti diwali diya puja will be normal after 6 PM

Diwali
On Sat amavasya will set on after 2:18 PM and accordingly diwali puja in offices ideally should not be done before that. Amavasya ends on Sunday at 10:36 AM.

Bhai Duj
Bhaiya duj is on Monday full day as Dwitiya starts from 7:05:04 AM onwards

Wishing everyone a very happy 5 days of Diwali and a wonderful corona free life ahead.

Conversation between Aadhar, Apple & Samsung

Aadhar - 
I have Biometric information of people who possess me 

Apple -
I too have the same 

Samsung -
I too have. So what's new? 

Aadhar -
But why do people criticise me? I only take it once from the people. 

Apple -
I take finger print and eye scan daily and people love giving it and pay Rs 50,000+ to buy me 

Aadhar-
OMG Why ? But they say someone can steal these biometric details from me. Don't they say to you ? 

Apple-ð 
Uff, I actually save every information on the IPhone backup Server. But they love me because I am costly.

Aadhar-
They say I am not secured, despite of the fact that I am encrypted 

Apple-
Look do you remember when FBI took data from us ? It's not about encryption. I gave all data to the US Intelligence 

Samsung-
I too ððð My brothers like Vivo, Xiomi do the same and the Chinese take it.

Aadhar-
But why only I am cursed? 

Apple and Samsung (unitedly)-
It is because you are Indian and possessed by Indian people who need excuses to abuse their own Government. You become that Excuse. We are brands! data.post:
For all my logical friends

A young man in his mid-twenties knocks on the door of a renowned Guru. He says: âIâve come to you because I wish to study Vedas.â

âDo you know Sanskrit?â the Guru asks.

âNo,â replies the young man.

âHave you studied any Indian philosophy?â

âNo. But donât worry. I just finished my doctoral dissertation at Harvard on logic. So now, I would just like to round up my education with a little study of the Vedas.â

âI doubt,â the Guru says, âthat you are ready to study Vedas. It is the deepest knowledge ever known. If you wish, however, I am willing to examine you in logic, and if you pass that test I will teach you Vedas.â

The young man agrees.

Guru holds up two fingers. âTwo men come down a chimney. One comes out with a clean face; the other comes out with a dirty face. Which one washes his face?â

The young man stares at the Guru. âIs that really a test in logic?â

The Guru nods.

âThe one with the dirty face washes his faceâ- he answers confidently.

âWrong. The one with the clean face washes his face. Examine the logic. The one with the dirty face looks at the one with the clean face and thinks his face is clean. The one with the clean face looks at the one with the dirty face and thinks his face is dirty. So, the one with the clean face washes his face.â

âVery clever,â the young man says. âGive me another test.â

The Guru again holds up two fingers. âTwo men come down a chimney. One comes out with a clean face, the other comes out with a dirty face. Which one washes his face?â

âWe have already established that. The one with the clean face washes his face.â

âWrong. Each one washes his face. Examine the logic. The one with the dirty face looks at the one with the clean face and thinks his face is clean. The one with the clean face looks at the one with the dirty face and thinks his face is dirty. So, the one with the clean face washes his face. When the one with the dirty face sees the one with the clean face wash his face, he also washes his face. So, each one washes his face.â

âI didnât think of that,â says the young man. Itâs shocking to me that I could make an error in logic. Test me again.â

The Guru holds up two fingers. âTwo men come down a chimney. One comes out with a clean face; the other comes out with a dirty face. Which one washes his face?â

âEach one washes his face.â

âWrong. Neither one washes his face. Examine the logic. The one with the dirty face looks at the one with the clean face and thinks his face is clean. The one with the clean face looks at the one with the dirty face and thinks his face is dirty. But when the one with the clean face sees the one with the dirty face doesnât wash his face, he also doesnât wash his face. So, neither one washes his face.â

The young man is desperate. âI am qualified to study Vedas. Please give me one more test.â

He groans, though, when the Guru lifts two fingers. âTwo men come down a chimney. One comes out with a clean face; the other comes out with a dirty face. Which one washes his face?â

âNeither one washes his face.â

âWrong. Do you now see why logic is an insufficient basis for studying Vedas? Tell me, how is it possible for two men to come down the same chimney, and for one to come out with a clean face and the other with a dirty face? Donât you see? The whole question is nonsense, foolishness, and if you spend your whole life trying to answer foolish questions, all your answers will be foolish, too.â

That's why having wisdom is more important than having logic.

I urge the parent community to not get carried away by some parents who stoke a popular sentiment to fulfil their unfulfilled political ambitions thru this agitation against school. If u think coolly u will see there are a set of people who hv a perennial problem with whatever the school does, for them the school can never do anything right but they will never exercise the option of trying to get their child into a better school (inherent contradiction u see). Go back to yr own school days and see that there were similar sentiments but today most if not all will agree that the school played a big part in whatever and wherever we are today. The same will apply to our kids and if we don't respect these institutions for a few bucks and chose to listen to some lumpen elements who stoke popular misbeliefs we will be making a huge mistake. Let's love, respect and show some reverence to the institutions (and teachers) where the future of not only our kids but the nation's future is being made.

Let's look the other way the right way.

data.post:
Huge respect for Bajaj Auto/Ravi Bajaj as they decide against pay cut - Buland Bharat ki Buland Tasveer.

Letter to employees
Dear Colleagues, Friends,

'The heart usually knows what to do. The challenge is to silence the mind'.

Today, in the context of the proposed organisation wide voluntary wage cut for 15th - 30th Apr 2020, we discussed the argument for reciprocity in the relationship between an organisation & it's employees ie that just as both share in the gain in the good times so must they also share in the pain in the bad times.

Equally we asked ourselves whether a large & profitable company like Bajaj Auto ought not to cushion against the impact of this pandemic & the associated lockdown it's people who have earned it the distinction of becoming the World's Favourite Indian, with industry leading metrics.

We have come to the conclusion that this is a time when we must act more from our hearts than from our minds.

Our business depends first & foremost upon each & every one of us, from the top to the bottom. Thus before we reach out to support our larger society, we are obliged to first secure our own; so long as we can help it, not a single child of the last contract worker must sleep hungry on our watch.

We have hence concluded that everyone including all of the contract labour engaged by us will be paid their full salary for Apr 2020. We will next review this matter towards the end of May taking into account the degree of positive impact on our business of the implementation of the progressive unlocking that is anticipated.

So for the moment, without the added burden of any wage related financial stress, we can hopefully better focus all of our energy on the safety & well being of our families.

And then, as soon as we're open for business, we shall as always as one spirited team have Bajaj Auto roaring back upon global roads full throttle!

We send you our love, prayers, & good wishes.
Human Resources.
Read more at:
https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/industry/auto/two-wheelers-three-wheelers/bajaj-auto-reverses-decision-to-cut-wages-all-employees-to-be-paid-in-full/articleshow/75516489.cms?utm_source=contentofinterest&utm_medium=text&utm_campaign=cppst

Trust begets Trust â its time to just do it

It is time for Govt of India/Modiji to repay the trust people have shown by:

1) Being in lockdown and making this huge economic sacrifice. Modiji pls remember people did it bcoz they trust you and u have tested us with Thali and Diya. 

2) Accepting everything you have said at face value no matter how difficult it is for us and even when we do not know what the future has in store for us. Forget minor deviations here and there but largely all of us have followed what you have prescribed without question.

3) Contributing generously to the PM cares fund.

Coming to suggestions

1) Now itâs time to repose the trust back in the people, you have an example to follow, just follow the steps that PV Narsimha Rao took in 1991. 

2) Trust people, we trust you and yr govt needs to trust us. While the relationship of trust between you and people is in-tact but we cannot say the same for yr agencies be it FM, RBI, CBI, Tax Department (biggest culprit), the commercial banks (2nd only to Tax Deptt), 

3) Now that lockdown has ensured that InfoTech flows to grassroots level, make the best use of it to use IT to solve common problems, initiate judicial reforms, simplify tax, laws. 

4) Make trusting people central to policy-making. 

5) It's a historic moment for you NaMo and not only India entire world is looking at you. 

If there was any time this is it and if anyone can do it is you.

data.text:
Recycling some old but good stuff 

When Grandma Goes To Court 

Lawyers should never ask a Mississippi grandma a question if they aren't prepared for the answer. 

In a trial, a Southern small-town prosecuting attorney called his first witness, a grandmotherly, elderly woman to the stand. He approached her and asked "Mrs. Jones, do you know me?" She responded, "Why yes I do know you since you were a little boy, and frankly you've been a big disappointment to me. You lie, you cheat on your wife, and you manipulate people and talk about them behind their backs. You think your a big shot when you haven't the brains to realize you'll never amount to anything more than a two-bit paper pusher. Yes I know you. 

The lawyer was stunned. Not knowing what else to do, he pointed across the room and asked, Mrs. Jones, do you know the defense attorney? 

She replied, "Why yes I do. I've known Mr. Bradley since he was a youngster, too. He's lazy, bigoted, and he has a drinking problem, He can't build a normal relationship with anyone, and his law practice is one of the worst in the entire state. Not to mention he cheated on his wife with three different women. One of them was your wife. Yes, I know him." 

The defense attorney nearly died. 

The judge asked both counselors to approach the bench and, in a very quiet voice said: 

"If either of you idiots asks her if she knows me, I'll send you both behind the bars."

data.post:
Bongo Maata a poem by Rabindranath Tagore. 

Punye paape dukkhe shukhe potone utthaney

Manush hoite dao tomar shontaney

Hey sneharto Bangobhumi, tobo grihokrore

Chiroshishu kore aar rakhiyo na dhorey.

Desh-deshantor majhe jar jetha sthaan

Khunjiya loitey dao koriya shondhan.

Podey podey choto choto nishedher dorey

Bendhe bendhe rakhiyo na bhalo chele korey.

Pran diye, dukkho shoye, aponar haathey

Shongraam koritey dao bhalo mondo-shathe.

Sheerno, shaanto, sadhu tobo putroder dhorey

Dao shobe grihochara, lokkhichara korey.

Shaath koti shontanere, he mugdho jononi,

Rekhecho Bangali korey, manush koroni.

Mother Bengal

Through good deeds and wrong, in sorrow and happiness, downfall and uprise,

Allow your children to flourish and grow, to full human potential realise.

Oh affectionate mother, in your lap most comforting â

As children you must not keep them forever nursing.

Across the world, wherever their places may be

To truly find themselves, you must set them free.

With a hundred little rules that cannot be understood

Do not force blind conformity for just the sake of being good

They must learn to stake life and face torment strong

To find out how to take the right along with the wrong

Grab your good natured sons and push them out there

The world is theirs for the taking, let them show no fear

This great multitude of children, Oh doting mother

Confining them to a Bengali identity, you have not let them discover the greater in humanity.

Indian Banks Association has issued an appeal stating from 23 March onwards, bank branches in India will be open only for essential services - cash deposits and withdrawals, clearing of cheques, remittances government transactions. All non-essential services will be suspended.

The chief secretaries of all states where confirmed novel coronavirus cases have been reported have been instructed by the Centre to extend restriction on movement of non-essential passengers till 31 March. data.text:
On his first visit to London City, Alastair MacGregor goes into the Ritzy Glitz Restaurant and treats himself to a huge meal with a bottle of champagne, finishing up with a Havana cigar.
Finally the waiter brings the bill on a silver tray. It comes to Â£99.99 so Alastair pays him with a one hundred pound note.
About five minutes later, he calls the waiter back and asks for his change.
Without altering his expression, the waiter leaves and returns a moment later with the silver tray.On it is a cent and a pack of condoms.
MacGregor is shocked and demands an explanation.
The waiter lifts his nose in the air, and says,
"Sir, it is the policy of our restaurant to encourage customers like you not to reproduce!â

data.post:
Dhoni was the captain when Sourav Ganguly retired from International cricket in 2008. 

Everybody remembers MS Dhoni's gesture when he asked to Ganguly to captain the side on the final day of the last test match. However, Ganguly has revealed that he had refused Dhoni's request to captain the side once. 

"As the match came to a close, Mahendra Singh Dhoni in a surprise gesture asked me to lead. I had rejected his offer earlier in the day, but could not refuse a second time." 

"But I must admit, at that stage, I found it difficult to focus. So after three overs I handed it back to Dhoni saying, it is your job, MS. We both smiled."

Today Ganguly is in a position to influence the final stages of Dhoni's career and he says - 'What matters is what Dhoni wants'

data.post:
A Company earning an additional income of 10 lacs will pay ~2.5 lacs as tax (after deducting all expenses incurred towards earning that income) and ~1.7 lacs if it is newly formed.
Other non-corporates (Firm/LLP/AoP/BoI) will have to pay ~3 lacs

An individual with a taxable income of 10 lacs or more will have to pay at least 3 lacs or more depending on his slab and without any deduction towards earning that income.

Let us hope there is something in the pipelines for the Individuals and SalHarried class

data.post:
True Story on Confidence

I was in first year of college, and my brother's best friend was in same college in last year. I knew him since I was in school. One day we were in canteen discussing something and out of nowhere the most beautiful girl of our college sat next to our table.

He was staring at her while we were talking. I noticed it and decided to have some fun.

ME :âdude don't even think about it, she is way out of your league. Just stop staring at herâ (i said that because he was an average looking guy, and the girl was really pretty.) To which he replied.

HIM : â Nothing is out my league, I can get anything I want. its just that she looks kind of sad, that's why I was looking at herâ. (I thought he was just trying to act cool and don't really have guts to talk to her.) So challenged him

ME : âyou think that girl is sad? Then go and talk to her, and if you make her smile I will lose 1000 rs right nowâ.

HIM : â Congratulation you just lost 1000 rs. Let's goâ. But only I will speak you'll stay silent.

We both approached the girls table. He stood next to her . and the conversation went like this.

HIM :â HI can we sit here. I have observed something about which I need to tell you. Its really important for you to know this.â

GIRL : â leave me alone, I'm not in mood for that. just go to your tableâ.

HIM : please just give me five minutes. Its really important for me to say this. It has something to do with you.

GIRL :( in angry tone) â OK what is it? Just tell me and go.

HIM : â Do you know about Fermi paradoxâ?.

GIRL : â Ya I know. But why are you asking me about it? What it has to do with me?

HIM : â It has everything to do with you.See the Fermi paradox, or Fermi's paradox, named after physicist Enrico Fermi, is the apparent contradiction between the lack of evidence and high probability for the existence of extraterrestrial civilizations. The basic points of the argument, made by physicists Enrico Fermi are: There are billions of stars in the galaxy that are similar to the Sun, and many of these stars are billions of years older than the Solar system. With high probability, some of these stars have Earth-like planets, and if the Earth is typical, some may have developed intelligent life. Some of these civilizations may have developed interstellar travel, a step the Earth is investigating now. Even at the slow pace of currently envisioned interstellar travel, the Milky Way galaxy could be completely traversed in a few million years.

Me and the girl looked at each in confusion. Both of us didn't know what he was talking about. It was embarrassing.

GIRL : â WTF are you talking about? Stop irritating me. Just tell me what it has to do with me.

HIM : My point is according to âFermi paradoxâ there could be life everywhere in milky way galaxy. But we are yet to find it. And we don't know if we will ever be able to find it. Similarly what I observed is that there could be millions of others girls on planet earth. And I'm not going to see them all, so I will never know. But right now, as of this moment I can say one thing. YOU ARE THE MOST BEAUTIFUL GIRL I HAVE EVER SEEN. Thats it. I just want you to know that. Thank you for your time.

I was shocked. After saying that he stands up and starts walking. I followed him. I didn't look at the girl to see her reaction. After walking few meters away from table, he said â now turn back and look at herâ. When I turned back to see her, what I saw was just amazing.

The girl was not only smiling she was blushing in shyness. I told him her reaction and he just smiled and asked me to pay him 1000 rs. Which I never paid actually. I didn't have 1000 rs. But few days later I came to know that they started dating.

I don't know how he came up with that Fermi paradox story. But the confidence he had while he was saying it to her without knowing what her reaction might be was just amazing. 6 years later in 2016 they got married and last month they had a daughter. The confidence he showed just to win a challenge, actually won him a soul mate and a beautiful family.

I tried that Fermi paradox story so many times, And trust me it works every single time. And I'm yet to pay him 1000 rs.

data.post:
A seven-year-old boy was at the centre of the courtroom drama yesterday at the Karnataka High Court. 

When he challenged a court ruling over who should have custody of him. The boy has a history of being beaten by his parents and the judge initially awarded custody to his aunt, in keeping with child custody law and regulations requiring that family unity be maintained to the degree possible. The boy surprised the court when he proclaimed that his aunt beat him more than his parents and he adamantly refused to live with her.
When the judge then suggested that he live with his grandparents, the boy cried out that they also beat him.
After considering the remainder of the immediate family and learning that domestic violence was apparently a way of life among them, The judge took the unprecedented step of allowing the boy to propose who should have custody of him. After two recesses to check legal references and confer with child welfare officials,

The judge granted temporary custody to the Royal Challengers Bangalore, whom the boy firmly believes are currently not capable of beating anyone.

data.post:
An Airbus 380 is on its way across Atlantic. It flies consistently at 800km/h in 30,000 ft. when suddenly a Eurofighter jet appears. The pilot of the fighter jet slows down, flies alongside the Airbus and greets the pilot of the passenger plane y radio: "Airbus flight, a boring flight isn't it? Take care and have a look here" He rolls his jet on his back, accelerates, breaks through the sound barrier, rises rapidly to height, only to stoop down almost to sea level. He loops back next to Airbus and asks" Well, how was that?" The Airbus pilot answers: "Very impressive, but now have a look here!" The jet pilot watches the Airbus, but nothing happens. It continues to fly stubbornly straight, at the same speed. After 5 minutes, the Airbus pilot radioed " Well, what are you saying now?" The jet pilot asks confused: "What did you do?" The other laughs and says, "I got up, stretched by legs, went to back of the flight to bathroom, got a cup of coffee & a cinnamon cake.

All jobs are great! One needs to see which job resonates with the you. If you are high on adrenaline, fighter jet might be your choice, but if you want comfort and peace, Airbus is the one!
Choose wisely!

title: Pawan Lohia shared a link.

data.text:
In a mother's womb were two babies. One asked the other: "Do you believe in life after delivery?" The other replies, "why, of course. There has to be something after delivery. Maybe we are here to prepare ourselves for what we will be later. "Nonsense," says the other. "There is no life after delivery. What would that life be?" "I don't know, but there will be more light than here. Maybe we will walk with our legs and eat from our mouths."
The other says "This is absurd! Walking is impossible. And eat with our mouths? Ridiculous. The umbilical cord supplies nutrition. Life after delivery is to be excluded. The umbilical cord is too short." "I think there is something and maybe it's different than it is here." the other replies, "No one has ever come back from there. Delivery is the end of life, and in the after-delivery it is nothing but darkness and anxiety and it takes us nowhere."
"Well, I don't know," says the other, "but certainly we will see mother and she will take care of us." "Mother??" You believe in mother? Where is she now? "She is all around us. It is in her that we live. Without her there would not be this world." "I don't see her, so it's only logical that she doesn't exist." To which the other replied, "sometimes when you're in silence you can hear her, you can perceive her." I believe there is a reality after delivery and we are here to prepare ourselves for that reality.

Resemblance..??? Anyone...??

data.post:
A must read 

Conversation between Aadhar, Apple & Samsung

Aadhar - 
I have Biometric information of people who possess me 

Apple -
I too have the same 

Samsung -
I too have. So what's new? 

Aadhar -
But why do people criticise me? I only take it once from the people. 

Apple -
I take finger print and eye scan daily and people love giving it and pay Rs 50,000+ to buy me 

Aadhar-
OMG Why ? But they say someone can steal these biometric details from me. Don't they say to you ? 

Apple-ð 
Uff, I actually save every information on the IPhone backup Server. But they love me because I am costly.

Aadhar-
They say I am not secured, despite of the fact that I am encrypted 

Apple-
Look do you remember when FBI took data from us ? It's not about encryption. I gave all data to the US Intelligence 

Samsung-
I too ððð My brothers like Vivo, Xiomi do the same and the Chinese take it.

Aadhar-
But why only I am cursed? 

Apple and Samsung (unitedly)-
It is because you are Indian and possessed by Indian people who need excuses to abuse their own Government. You become that Excuse. We are brands! People give Rs 70K- 80 K and give all details like Location, GPS, Contacts, Biometric data, every damn thing and love posting on Facebook - Hey, That's my New IPhone, my new Samsung!!

data.post:
An anecdote on media

Mohammed Mahathir was then the PM of Malaysia. He was being heavily criticized by the Singapore media -never mind what he did.
So he called an informal meeting with the Editors of the Singapore newspapers and asked," What do I have to do for you to stop demonizing me?"'
So the Editor of Strait Times said ," You must walk on water and cross from Johor Bahru into Singapore. Then, you will be forgiven."
Next morning Mahathir prayed to God and performed the miracle and as cameras clicked away and videos rolled Mahathir walked on water and entered Singapore.
Next morning the Strait Times had this headline: "Mahathir cannot Swim".
The Business Times said," Mahathir illegally crosses into Singapore without completing immigration formalities."
Mahathir said,"You bastards I give up."

data.post:
Late last year, two young men decided to live a month of their lives on the income of an average poor Indian. One of them, Tushar, the son of a police officer in Haryana, studied at the University of Pennsylvania and worked for three years as an investment banker in the US and Singapore. The other, Matt, migrated as a teenager to the States with his parents, and studied in MIT. Both decided at different points to return to India, joined the UID Project in Bengaluru, came to share a flat, and became close friends.

The idea suddenly struck them one day. Both had returned to India in the vague hope that they could be of use to their country. But they knew the people of this land so little. Tushar suggested one evening â âLet us try to understand an âaverage Indian', by living on an âaverage income'.â His friend Matt was immediately captured by the idea. They began a journey which would change them forever.

To begin with, what was the average income of an Indian? They calculated that India's Mean National Income was Rs. 4,500 a month, or Rs. 150 a day. Globally people spend about a third of their incomes on rent. Excluding rent, they decided to spend Rs. 100 each a day. They realised that this did not make them poor, only average. Seventy-five per cent Indians live on less than this average.

The young men moved into the tiny apartment of their domestic help, much to her bemusement. What changed for them was that they spent a large part of their day planning and organising their food. Eating out was out of the question; even dhabas were too expensive. Milk and yoghurt were expensive and therefore used sparingly, meat was out of bounds, as were processed food like bread. No ghee or butter, only a little refined oil. Both are passionate cooks with healthy appetites. They found soy nuggets a wonder food â affordable and high on proteins, and worked on many recipes. Parle G biscuits again were cheap: 25 paise for 27 calories! They innovated a dessert of fried banana on biscuits. It was their treat each day.

Restricted life

Living on Rs.100 made the circle of their life much smaller. They found that they could not afford to travel by bus more than five km in a day. If they needed to go further, they could only walk. They could afford electricity only five or six hours a day, therefore sparingly used lights and fans. They needed also to charge their mobiles and computers. One Lifebuoy soap cut into two. They passed by shops, gazing at things they could not buy. They could not afford the movies, and hoped they would not fall ill.

However, the bigger challenge remained. Could they live on Rs. 32, the official poverty line, which had become controversial after India's Planning Commission informed the Supreme Court that this was the poverty line for cities (for villages it was even lower, at Rs. 26 per person per day)?

Harrowing experience

For this, they decided to go to Matt's ancestral village Karucachal in Kerala, and live on Rs. 26. They ate parboiled rice, a tuber and banana and drank black tea: a balanced diet was impossible on the Rs. 18 a day which their briefly adopted âpoverty' permitted. They found themselves thinking of food the whole day. They walked long distances, and saved money even on soap to wash their clothes. They could not afford communication, by mobile and internet. It would have been a disaster if they fell ill. For the two 26-year-olds, the experience of âofficial poverty' was harrowing.

Yet, when their experiment ended with Deepavali, they wrote to their friends: âWish we could tell you that we are happy to have our ânormal' lives back. Wish we could say that our sumptuous celebratory feast two nights ago was as satisfying as we had been hoping for throughout our experiment. It probably was one of the best meals we've ever had, packed with massive amounts of love from our hosts. However, each bite was a sad reminder of the harsh reality that there are 400 million people in our country for whom such a meal will remain a dream for quite some time. That we can move on to our comfortable life, but they remain in the battlefield of survival â a life of tough choices and tall constraints. A life where freedom means little and hunger is plenty...

Plenty of questions

It disturbs us to spend money on most of the things that we now consider excesses. Do we really need that hair product or that branded cologne? Is dining out at expensive restaurants necessary for a happy weekend? At a larger level, do we deserve all the riches we have around us? Is it just plain luck that we were born into circumstances that allowed us to build a life of comfort? What makes the other half any less deserving of many of these material possessions, (which many of us consider essential) or, more importantly, tools for self-development (education) or self-preservation (healthcare)?

We don't know the answers to these questions. But we do know the feeling of guilt that is with us now. Guilt that is compounded by the love and generosity we got from people who live on the other side, despite their tough lives. We may have treated them as strangers all our lives, but they surely didn't treat us as that way...â

So what did these two friends learn from their brief encounter with poverty? That hunger can make you angry. That a food law which guarantees adequate nutrition to all is essential. That poverty does not allow you to realise even modest dreams. And above all â in Matt's words â that empathy is essential for democracy.

This post is Courtesy my friend - Biren Parekh
